name: Livermorium
name_origin: Named in honor of Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory in Livermore, California, which collaborated with JINR Dubna on the discovery.

discovery.discoverer: Joint Institute for Nuclear Research (JINR) Dubna team led by Yuri Oganessian, in collaboration with Lawrence Livermore National Laboratory

occurrence.abundance_summary: Livermorium does not occur naturally. It is a synthetic element produced in particle accelerators via the hot-fusion reaction 48Ca + 248Cm.
states: synthetic, produced atom-by-atom in particle accelerators

reactivity_summary: No chemistry of livermorium has been experimentally characterized. By analogy with its lighter group 16 homologs (tellurium, polonium) it is predicted to be a post-transition metal with accessible +2 and +4 oxidation states; relativistic effects are expected to stabilize the +2 state and reduce the prevalence of higher oxidation states.
isotopes_summary: All livermorium isotopes are radioactive. The longest-lived confirmed isotope is 293Lv with a half-life of about 50-60 milliseconds; its alpha-decay chain produces flerovium, copernicium, and lighter superheavy isotopes.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Royal Society of Chemistry - Periodic Table: Livermorium
